critic_raw:
The stratified random split for 5-year continuous sensor time-series data is a fundamental error. Sensor observations from the same machine within a 7-day window before a failure will be split between training and test. The RNN will see some of these high-risk windows during training and encounter their temporal neighbors in the test set. Because the 7-day prediction window creates a dense zone of positive labels approaching each failure event, randomly split positive examples from the same machine's pre-failure trajectory will appear in both train and test, directly leaking temporal failure proximity into evaluation.

Preprocessing is fit on the entire dataset before splitting (explicitly stated). For sensor data, this means rolling statistics (1-hour, 24-hour means and standard deviations) are computed using global normalization parameters that include test-period sensor baselines. If machines exhibit baseline sensor drift over 5 years (which the design acknowledges with per-sensor normalization), computing normalization globally across all time periods smooths this drift in a way that is unavailable at deployment time.

The SPC baseline is evaluated at default settings (control limit multiplier 2.0, three consecutive violations) without hyperparameter optimization on the validation set, while the RNN undergoes extensive grid search over learning rate, LSTM units, dropout, and batch size. The comparison is therefore between an optimized RNN and an unoptimized SPC baseline. This asymmetry favors the RNN regardless of true relative performance.

The stated hypothesis claims the RNN 'reduces unplanned equipment downtime by 25%' but the primary evaluation metric is F1 score on a labeled dataset of sensor windows. F1 score improvement does not translate to percentage downtime reduction without additional assumptions about the intervention response rate, how early warnings are acted upon, and what fraction of failures are preventable. The 25% downtime reduction claim cannot be empirically evaluated by this experiment design.

The post-hoc simulation for 'downtime hours averted' uses fixed constants (8 hours prevented per true positive, 2 hours wasted per false positive, 48 hours per false negative) that are not measured from the data but assumed. Small changes in these constants can reverse the apparent business-case superiority of the RNN.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split of 5-year sensor time-series data allows observations from the same machine's pre-failure degradation trajectory to appear in both training and test sets — positive examples within the 7-day prediction window are temporally autocorrelated and their random splitting directly leaks failure proximity.

All preprocessing transformers including per-sensor rolling statistics are fit on the full dataset before splitting — normalization parameters reflecting machine baselines across all 5 years are unavailable at actual deployment time, constituting feature-level leakage.

The SPC baseline uses default untuned parameters (multiplier 2.0, 3 consecutive violations) while the RNN is extensively hyperparameter-searched — this asymmetric optimization effort systematically favors the RNN regardless of true relative performance.

The hypothesis claims 25% downtime reduction but the experiment measures F1 score on labeled windows — F1 improvement cannot be mapped to percentage downtime reduction without measured intervention response rates and failure preventability fractions, so the stated hypothesis is not testable by this design.

The business-value simulation uses assumed fixed cost constants (8/2/48 hours) that are not derived from plant data — small changes in these constants can reverse the apparent superiority of the ML approach.
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split of 5-year sensor time-series data allows observations from the same machine's pre-failure degradation trajectory to appear in both training and test sets — positive examples within the 7-day prediction window are temporally autocorrelated and their random splitting directly leaks failure proximity.

All preprocessing transformers including per-sensor rolling statistics are fit on the full dataset before splitting — normalization parameters reflecting machine baselines across all 5 years are unavailable at actual deployment time, constituting feature-level leakage.

The SPC baseline uses default untuned parameters (multiplier 2.0, 3 consecutive violations) while the RNN is extensively hyperparameter-searched — this asymmetric optimization effort systematically favors the RNN regardless of true relative performance.

The hypothesis claims 25% downtime reduction but the experiment measures F1 score on labeled windows — F1 improvement cannot be mapped to percentage downtime reduction without measured intervention response rates and failure preventability fractions, so the stated hypothesis is not testable by this design.

The business-value simulation uses assumed fixed cost constants (8/2/48 hours) that are not derived from plant data — small changes in these constants can reverse the apparent superiority of the ML approach.
